[JS Weird World] No.25 JSON and Object Literals — They're Closer Than You Think

There's a common misunderstanding about what JSON really is, so let's clear it up. JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation, and it was inspired by JavaScript object literals. When you create an object literal, you're basically creating something that looks a lot like JSON.

Back in the day, data was usually sent in a format that looked something like this:
language-xml shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

The problem is, once the data starts to grow, this gets really inefficient. The file gets huge because it's packed with unnecessary symbols and a ton of repeated tags, and all of that hurts speed. That's why people came up with a leaner approach (JSON):
That's the reason we now use JSON to send and receive data. But JSON's format is
— for one, the keys have to be wrapped in quotes:
language-json shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

Here's the thing the course points out: JSON syntax is valid object literal syntax, but not all object literal syntax is valid JSON, because JSON is a stricter format. So JSON isn't actually part of JavaScript — but it's super popular in JavaScript, so JavaScript gives us two methods to convert between JSON and object literals.

A lot of people misunderstand what JSON actually is. So what is it? JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation, and it was inspired by JavaScript object literals — when you write an object literal, you're basically writing something that looks a lot like JSON. In this post we'll clear up the common confusion and cover JSON.stringify() and JSON.parse().
